sections.heading: Core Concept
sections.content: A Binary Search Tree is a [[primary|divide-and-conquer]] structure — like flipping through a phone book, each comparison eliminates half the remaining nodes. Every **node** strictly follows the [[primary|left-smaller, right-larger]] rule.
items: Strict ordering: a **node**'s **left subtree** holds only smaller values, the **right subtree** holds only larger values, maintaining the [[primary|left-smaller, right-larger]] invariant throughout
Recursive property: every **subtree** must itself be a valid BST, and [[primary|duplicate keys]] are generally not permitted
Efficient operations: like binary search on an array, each step downward eliminates half the search space, achieving [[primary|logarithmic time]] lookup

sections.heading: Operation Complexity
sections.content: Performance depends entirely on tree height. A balanced tree halves the data at each step, but a degenerate tree that collapses into a one-sided linked list sees performance drop sharply.
items: Search: compare keys and walk downward — average [[success|O(log n)]], worst-case degrades to [[danger|O(n)]]
Insert: traverse to the correct empty position — average [[success|O(log n)]], worst-case degrades to [[danger|O(n)]]
Delete: locate a predecessor or successor based on child count — average [[success|O(log n)]], worst-case degrades to [[danger|O(n)]]

sections.heading: What Can Be Optimized
sections.content: The standard structure handles random data well, but struggles with **sorted input**, which causes it to degenerate into an inefficient **linked list**. Switch to **AVL Trees** or **Red-Black Trees** — **self-balancing BST** variants — to address this.
items: Great for dynamic management: ideal for datasets with frequent **searches**, **inserts**, and **deletes** on random data while maintaining strong performance
Great for maintaining order: **inorder traversal** instantly produces a sorted sequence, useful for dictionaries and **priority queues**
Poor with sorted input: inserting ascending or descending data in sequence causes severe skew and degrades to an **O(n)** **linked list**
Optimized alternatives: to guard against imbalance, switch to **AVL Trees** or other **self-balancing BSTs** to guarantee **logarithmic time** performance

sections.heading: When to Use a BST
sections.content: Reach for a BST when a problem requires maintaining a dynamically changing data stream with frequent inserts and deletes, while also needing fast lookup of extremes, specific ranks, or nearest neighbors.
items: Problems requiring dynamic lookup of a specific rank, such as **K-th Smallest Element (Select K)**
Scenarios needing fast retrieval and removal of both maximum and minimum values — a **double-ended priority queue**
Problems counting smaller elements to the right of each element in a dynamic array — **Count Smaller Elements**
Problems dynamically finding the smallest value greater than the current element — **Smallest Greater Element**

sections.heading: Node Deletion Mechanics
sections.content: Deletion is more complex than insertion — it must be handled differently depending on how many children the target node has. The **two-children** case, which requires finding a replacement, is the most involved and the most common sticking point for beginners.
items: When the target has **no children**, simply remove the **leaf node** — no structural repair needed, [[success|O(1)]] extra work
When the target has **one child**, remove the node and link its single child directly to the parent — [[success|O(1)]] extra work
When the target has **two children**, spend [[warning|O(h)]] finding the **right subtree minimum** or **left subtree maximum** to replace it

sections.heading: Self-Balancing Search Trees
sections.content: The standard BST degenerates into a **linked list** under extreme input. In practice, variants like **AVL Trees** and **Red-Black Trees** are used to guarantee [[success|O(log n)]] performance under any conditions.
items: Inserting already-sorted data causes severe skew, degrading performance to [[danger|O(n)]]
In practice, **AVL Trees** and **Red-Black Trees** — **self-balancing BSTs** — are used to prevent degeneration
When the tree becomes unbalanced, **tree rotations** and color-flip operations automatically restore balance
Regardless of how data is inserted or deleted, lookup, insert, and delete all stay at [[success|O(log n)]]
